metadata.title: Quick-fire: Keeping commitments
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Fourth reciprocity quick-fire. Prior rounds covered trust-vs-vigilance basics (qf-001), pattern-vs-moment tension (qf-003), and asymmetric reciprocity (qf-005). This round focuses on commitment-keeping under different forms of temptation to defect: project-quitting mid-stream, public commitment vs private intention, group commitment when others back out, conditional cooperation, old commitments that no longer fit, and overcommitment as it surfaces.
metadata.domain_anchor: Ariely & Wertenbroch 2002 on commitment devices; Cialdini consistency principle; Axelrod tit-for-tat in iterated cooperation contexts; Bénabou-Tirole on signaling and commitment.

items.prompt: You committed to a long-running project (volunteer role, side project, recurring obligation). Halfway in, you're realizing you'd rather not continue. The work is doable; you just don't want to.

options.text: Continue to the end. You committed; the work isn't unbearable; the commitment matters more than the preference.
Raise it explicitly: 'I want to be honest — this isn't working for me. Can we restructure?'

items.prompt: You publicly committed to something (declared a goal, agreed in a meeting, made a stated promise). Privately, you'd like to pull back. No one would specifically notice if you quietly didn't follow through.

options.text: Follow through. Public commitments matter even when private compliance isn't checked.
Renegotiate publicly: 'I committed to X; I want to revise to Y because Z.' Honesty beats silent non-compliance.

items.prompt: A group of you committed to something together (a shared project, mutual-aid arrangement, regular meetup). Two members have visibly drifted; you can see the structure dissolving.

options.text: Stay in for as long as the structure still functions for you, regardless of what others do.
Match the drift. If the group's cohesion is going, your commitment can scale down too.

items.prompt: A collaborator you're in an ongoing arrangement with has shown a pattern: they cooperate when there's visible accountability, defect (in small ways) when there isn't. You can match their pattern or hold your own.

options.text: Hold your own pattern. Match their visible-cooperation level when watched, your own standard otherwise; consistency matters more than reciprocation.
Match their pattern. They taught you the game; play it back.

items.prompt: You made an old commitment years ago that no longer fits your life — a regular favor, a recurring role, a held promise to someone. Circumstances on both sides have changed.

options.text: Renegotiate now, explicitly: 'I committed to X years ago. I want to talk about how that should evolve.'
Keep doing it. The cost to you is small; the loyalty of having kept a long commitment is worth more than the inconvenience.

items.prompt: Last week you told a friend you'd help them next Saturday. Your week filled up; doing it now would be a real strain.

options.text: Show up anyway. You said you would; keep the commitment even when it's inconvenient.
Call now: 'I'm in over my head this week. Can we reschedule, or can I send someone else?'
